pattern: Depth-first order falls out of a stack for free: process a node the moment you reach it, then dive into its children before ever touching a sibling.
hints: A recursive visit-then-recurse-on-each-child function already produces preorder — the hard part is trusting it, not building it.
To go iterative, push a node's children onto an explicit stack so the leftmost child is the next one popped — that means pushing them in reverse order.
Pop a node, record its value, then push all of its children rightmost-first, so the next pop is always the leftmost unvisited child.

edge_inputs.description: an empty tree has no values to visit
a lone root visits just itself
one root fanning out to nine children in a flat row, testing left-to-right child order
a single-child chain six nodes deep, where preorder and postorder both degenerate to the same sequence
an irregular tree with uneven fan-out, testing that a stack-based walk still dives depth-first before moving to the next sibling
repeated values across separate nodes so an identity bug (e.g. skipping a 'seen' value) would drop a node

stress.description: random n-ary trees grown by attaching each new node to a random existing node with up to 4 children
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 80)))
    children = {1: []}
    order = [1]
    for v in range(2, n + 1):
        candidates = [p for p in order if len(children[p]) < 4]
        parent = rng.choice(candidates) if candidates else rng.choice(order)
        children[parent].append(v)
        children[v] = []
        order.append(v)
    wire = [1, None]
    queue = [1]
    while queue:
        node = queue.pop(0)
        for k in children[node]:
            wire.append(k)
            queue.append(k)
        wire.append(None)
    while wire and wire[-1] is None:
        wire.pop()
    return [wire]
